did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study used a unified dual convolutional neural network (CNN) deep learning algorithm to classify non-melanoma skin cancer (NMSC) from dermoscopic and smartphone images. The model combined a visual classifier with a sonification classifier to predict malignancy, achieving specific sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy metrics for NMSC diagnosis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL ARTICLE' and presents original experimental results from a study comparing dermoscopic and smartphone images using a unified dual convolutional neural network (CNN). It includes specific Methods, Results (with metrics like AUC, sensitivity, specificity, and p-values), and a Conclusion based on new data analysis, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Non‑melanoma skin cancer diagnosis: a comparison between dermoscopic and smartphone images by unified visual and sonification deep learning algorithms
journal: Journal of Cancer Research and Clinical Oncology
brief_summary: This study evaluated the diagnostic performance of a unified dual convolutional neural network (CNN) for non-melanoma skin cancer (NMSC) using dermoscopic images versus smartphone-captured images. The AI model combined a visual classifier with a sonification classifier to predict malignancy in histopathology-verified lesions. Data sources included the Ham10000 dataset, a previous clinical study, and a dedicated smartphone dataset, totaling 132 dermoscopic and 170 smartphone NMSC images. The main evaluation approach compared sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy metrics between the two image types. Key results showed that dermoscopic images achieved significantly higher sensitivity (95.5% vs 75.3%) and accuracy (0.88 vs 0.75) than smartphone images. The conclusion indicated that telemedicine use of smartphone images results in a substantial decrease in diagnostic performance compared to dermoscopy.

lesion_types_studied.details: Histopathology-verified malignant lesion. Included in dermoscopic dataset (n=96) and smartphone dataset (n=139). Classified as non-melanoma skin cancer (NMSC).
Histopathology-verified malignant lesion. Included in dermoscopic dataset (n=36) and smartphone dataset (n=31). Classified as non-melanoma skin cancer (NMSC).
Histopathology-verified benign lesion. Included in dermoscopic dataset (n=33) and smartphone dataset (n=28). Used as the benign comparator to NMSCs.

General category for non-malignant lesions. Total count of benign lesions was 33 for dermoscopic images and 28 for smartphone images. Excluded from specific histopathology breakdown in Table 1 but mentioned in Abstract and Methods.
Explicitly excluded from the study because these lesions are a 'gray zone definition and a preneoplastic entity' and often treated before excision, resulting in distorted morphological features.
Explicitly excluded due to 'major pigmentary nature'.
Mentioned in the introduction to define Non-Melanoma Skin Cancer (NMSC) by exclusion. Not included in the study's target classes or datasets.

all_task_types.target: Malignancy status (malignant vs. benign) of non-melanoma skin cancer lesions
all_task_types.details: The study uses a unified dual convolutional neural network (CNN) to classify histopathology-verified skin lesions into discrete diagnostic categories: malignant (Basal cell carcinoma, Squamous cell carcinoma) versus benign (Seborrheic keratosis). The model outputs a dichotomous prediction (excise or do not excise) based on a malignancy score derived from a softmax prediction layer. Performance was evaluated using sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, and AUC for binary classification.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017 Dataset
datasets_used.description: Publicly available dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration used for training the deep learning model. Included advanced dermoscope images and non-dermoscopic regular photos.

use.training: Used for training the CNN with a total of 4361 advanced dermoscope images and 800 non-dermoscopic regular photos.
use.other: Pretraining and feature representation for the unified dual deep learning algorithm.

datasets_used.name: Interactive Atlas of Dermoscopy (IAD) Dataset
datasets_used.description: Publicly available dataset used for training the deep learning model.

use.training: Used for training the CNN alongside the ISIC 2017 dataset.
use.other: Pretraining and feature representation for the unified dual deep learning algorithm.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 Dataset
datasets_used.description: Public dataset from which dermoscopic images were derived via 1:5 random selection.

use.testing: 149 images were randomly selected and used in the dermoscopic testing cohort.

datasets_used.name: Previous Prospective Clinical Study (NCT03362138)
datasets_used.description: Private dataset from a previous clinical study conducted by the authors, involving histopathology-verified images.

use.testing: 16 images were used in the dermoscopic testing cohort.

datasets_used.name: Pacheco et al. 2020 Dataset
datasets_used.description: Recently published and biopsy-validated dataset dedicated to Non-Melanoma Skin Cancer (NMSC), used for smartphone images.

use.testing: 159 images were randomly selected (1:3 ratio) and used in the smartphone testing cohort.

datasets_used.name: Journal of Investigative Dermatology Editorial Images 2018
datasets_used.description: Dataset of smartphone images used for the study.

use.testing: 39 images were used in the smartphone testing cohort.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: Area under the curve (AUC) of the receiver operating characteristic curve (ROC)

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To quantify diagnostic performance by calculating the Area Under the Curve (AUC) for malignancy scores in dermoscopic and smartphone datasets.
To derive sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, positive predictive value (precision), and negative predictive value (NPV) for both image types.
To compare accuracy, sensitivity, and negative predictive value between dermoscopic and smartphone diagnostic methods; also used to compare age between groups.
To compare gender distribution between the dermoscopic and smartphone image groups.
To estimate the uncertainty for AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and predictive values.
To evaluate sensitivity and specificity at a fixed threshold, following Cochrane review guidelines for comparing diagnostic techniques.
To summarize baseline and demographic characteristics (mean age, range, sex distribution).

hypothesis_testing.test: Unpaired Student’s t-test (for continuous variables like age, accuracy, sensitivity, NPV) and Chi-squared test (for categorical variables like gender)
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in the measured parameter (e.g., age, accuracy, sensitivity, gender distribution) between the dermoscopic image group and the smartphone image group.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in the measured parameter between the dermoscopic image group and the smartphone image group.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: A p-value < 0.05 was considered significant. The study found that dermoscopic images had significantly higher accuracy (p < 0.005) and sensitivity (p < 0.001) than smartphone images. There was no significant difference in specificity (p = NS) or demographic characteristics like age (p = 0.53) and gender (p = 0.58) between the groups.

Dermoscopic Images (DI).notes: Dermoscopic images were captured by dermatologists or trained GPs using digital and smartphone cameras. Both polarized and non-polarized images were included.

source_datasets: Pacheco et al. 2020 Dataset (n=159, 1:3 random selection)
Journal of Investigative Dermatology Editorial Images 2018 (n=39)
Smartphone Images (SI).notes: Smartphone images were captured by different non-standardized smartphone devices. Exclusion rates were 18.7% for non-dermoscopic images.

accuracy_comparison.finding: DI accuracy (0.88) was significantly higher than SI accuracy (0.75).

sensitivity_comparison.finding: DI sensitivity (95.5%) was significantly higher than SI sensitivity (75.3%).

specificity_comparison.finding: No significant difference in specificity between DI (57.6%) and SI (71.4%).

npv_comparison.finding: DI NPV (76.0%) significantly outperformed SI NPV (32.3%).

fixed_cutoff_analysis.description: Performance evaluated at fixed cutoff points of 80% for sensitivity or specificity.

architecture_used.main_model: Unified Dual Convolutional Neural Network (DUA) combining a visual classifier and a sonification classifier
architecture_used.pretraining: The visual CNN backbone was pretrained on the ISIC 2017 dataset (4361 advanced dermoscope images and 800 non-dermoscopic regular photos) and the Interactive Atlas of Dermoscopy (IAD) dataset. The sonification component utilized a K-means clustering algorithm on weighted activations from the penultimate layer of the visual classifier.
architecture_used.input_size: Raw images (dimensions not explicitly specified, but derived from dermoscopic and smartphone sources). Sonification input was an audio file derived from image-to-sound mapping.

components: Visual Classifier: CNN receiving raw image, trained to predict malignancy.
Sonification Classifier: CNN processing an audio file obtained by sonifying the original image (image-to-sound mapping).
Feature Extraction: Batch normalization used in the CNN. Weighted activations of all 1024 nodes in the penultimate layer were sonified.
Clustering: K-means clustering algorithm employed to cluster activations into groups; cluster centroids mapped to loudness, timbre, and duration.
architecture_details.fusion_method: The output (prediction) layers of both independent CNNs were removed. Two new fully connected layers were added on top, taking the outputs of the two models as inputs. A new softmax prediction layer was added to the fully connected layers to provide the final unified prediction. The unified structure was trained until convergence, with most training occurring in the fully connected layers while prior pretrained layers were fine-tuned.

training_details.training_data: ISIC 2017 dataset and IAD dataset for pretraining. Fine-tuning performed on the study's specific datasets.
training_details.augmentation: Data augmentation was performed as previously reported.
training_details.loss_function: Not reported (Softmax prediction layer used for final output)

classification_or_output_head.output: Dichotomous prediction (malignant vs. benign / excise or do not excise)
classification_or_output_head.details: The softmax layer provides the final unified prediction based on inputs from the two new fully connected layers.

preprocessing_techniques: Exclusion of images displaying whole organ appearance, ink markings, more than 15 hairs per field, scale bars extraneous to the capturing device, blurred images, and photos less than 200 Kb.
Random selection of images from source datasets (1:5 ratio for HAM10000, 1:3 ratio for Pacheco et al. 2020 dataset).
Triaging of datasets by an expert dermatologist applying exclusion criteria.
Sonification of weighted activations from the penultimate layer of the visual classifier using a K-means clustering algorithm to map cluster centroids to loudness, timbre, and duration.
Application of fixed cutoff points of 80% for sensitivity or specificity for comparative analysis.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Performed as previously reported in reference 14 (Walker et al. 2019). Specific techniques, probabilities, or ranges were not detailed in this paper.

conclusions: Telemedicine use of smartphone images results in a substantial decrease in diagnostic performance compared to dermoscopy, which needs to be considered by both healthcare providers and patients.
Dermoscopic images achieved significantly higher sensitivity (95.5% vs 75.3%, p < 0.001) and accuracy (0.88 vs 0.75, p < 0.005) than smartphone images.
There was no significant difference in specificity between dermoscopic and smartphone images (p = NS).
The negative predictive value of dermoscopic images (76.0%) significantly outperformed that of smartphone images (32.3%, p < 0.001).

The diagnostic gap between dermoscopy and smartphone images is attributed to constitutional differences in the skin layers analyzed, with dermoscopy uncovering sub-epidermal aspects critical for diagnosis.

The study limitations include a retrospective design, comparison between different groups of patients, and the use of a specific ratio of malignant to benign lesions derived from interrelated databases.
An ideal design would prospectively assess the same patient using both dermoscopy and smartphone images compared with histopathological reports.
important_remarks: The study has a retrospective design and compares different groups of patients rather than the same patients, which limits causal inference.
The dataset composition has a high ratio of malignant to benign lesions (approximately 5-7:1) derived from interrelated databases, which may not reflect real-world prevalence.
Dermoscopic images were captured by dermatologists or trained GPs using standardized equipment, whereas smartphone images were captured by non-standardized devices, introducing device bias.
The study excluded actinic keratosis, vascular lesions, and pigmented nevi, limiting the generalizability of the model to these common skin conditions.
The dermoscopic dataset (from HAM10000 and a previous clinical study) did not include ethnicity details, precluding demographic comparisons.
The CNN code is not publicly available, limiting reproducibility.
The study was partially funded by Bostel Technologies, indicating a potential conflict of interest.
Smartphone images had a lower negative predictive value (32.3%) compared to dermoscopic images (76.0%), indicating a high rate of missed NMSCs in the telemedicine setting.
The diagnostic gap is attributed to constitutional differences in skin layers analyzed, with dermoscopy uncovering sub-epidermal aspects critical for diagnosis that smartphone images miss.
